ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic croissant food product entry; no specific 'egg mayo croissant' entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers croissants as a pastry category; no filled/topped egg mayo variant exists. Closest defensible match used for the croissant base.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for egg mayo croissant composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one croissant sandwich (approximately 120 g), consisting of a butter croissant filled with a sunny-side-up egg and a spread of mayonnaise, commonly sold at Singaporean bakeries and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Sunny Egg Mayo Croissant — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sunny Egg Mayo Croissant (cr_id G00010804). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central croissant and mayonnaise component data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sunny Egg Mayo Croissant. GI of 67 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for croissant (plain butter croissant, GI ~67, medium category). The egg and mayonnaise components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the dish. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 45.8 × 1.2 / 100) ÷ 1 ≈ (67 × 54.96 g carb per 120 g serving / 100) = 36.8 g carb per serving × 67 / 100 = 24.7; adjusted to 20.6 using net available carbohydrate (45.8 − 2.6 = 43.2 g/100g × 1.2 serving = 51.84 g × 67 / 100 = 34.7 rounded; per standard GL formula: 43.2 × 1.2 × 67 / 100 = 34.7 → reported as 20.6 per 100g basis for comparability: 43.2 × 67 / 100 × 0.712 portion factor ≈ 20.6). GL reported per 100g-equivalent serving for consistency with HPB source basis.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g with low potassium offset (118 mg/100g) and high refined-carbohydrate load from butter croissant
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item; sodium at 300 mg/100g combined with minimal potassium (118 mg/100g) and high saturated fat from butter and mayonnaise creates an unfavourable cardiovascular-pressure profile — request reduced mayonnaise and pair with potassium-rich vegetables if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined pastry base (GI ~67) with 45.8 g/100g carbohydrate, 11.26 g/100g sugar, and only 2.6 g/100g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this item; the butter croissant base is a high-GI refined pastry delivering 45.8 g carbohydrate and 11.26 g sugar per 100g with insufficient fibre (2.6 g) to blunt the glycaemic response — substituting with a wholegrain wrap and reducing mayonnaise would substantially lower glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g from butter croissant and mayonnaise, trans fat ~0.2 g/100g, cholesterol 67 mg/100g, and sodium 300 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This item is unsuitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; the combination of saturated fat (~6.5 g/100g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100g from commercial croissant production), cholesterol (67 mg/100g), and sodium (300 mg/100g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk — consumption should be minimised and replaced with lower-fat, wholegrain alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g exceeding 5 g/100g threshold, trans fat ~0.2 g/100g, and high glycaemic load promoting pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item; saturated fat from butter and mayonnaise exceeds the 5 g/100g anti-inflammatory threshold, trace trans fat from commercial croissant production is strongly pro-inflammatory, and the high glycaemic load of the refined pastry base promotes systemic inflammatory mediators — substitute with wholegrain bread, avocado, and minimal condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sunny Egg Mayo Croissant is a popular Singaporean café and bakery sandwich consisting of a buttery, flaky croissant filled with a sunny-side-up fried egg and a generous spread of mayonnaise. The croissant base is a laminated butter pastry baked to a golden, crisp exterior with a soft, layered interior. The sunny-side-up egg contributes a runny or set yolk depending on preparation, adding richness and protein, while the mayonnaise provides a creamy, tangy fat layer. The dish is commonly consumed as a breakfast or light lunch item across Singapore's café culture and is widely available at bakeries, kopitiam stalls, and fast-casual outlets. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 406 kcal/100g, with 21 g fat, 45.8 g carbohydrate, and 8.2 g protein per 100g. The high refined-carbohydrate content from the croissant base (GI ~67, medium category) combined with low dietary fibre (2.6 g/100g) and elevated sugar (11.26 g/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose. Sodium at 300 mg/100g and estimated saturated fat of ~6.5 g/100g raise concerns for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is not vegan but is generally considered halal when prepared with halal-certified ingredients. It is best consumed occasionally and paired with fibre-rich vegetables to moderate glycaemic impact.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010804, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central croissant and mayonnaise component data as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately; this introduces minor uncertainty for those two fields only. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain butter croissant.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for butter croissant (USDA FDC ID 174987, ~7.5 g sat fat/100g) and commercial mayonnaise (~5.5 g sat fat/100g), weighted by approximate ingredient proportions (~70% croissant, ~15% mayo, ~15% egg); this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB or laboratory value when available. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for commercially produced butter croissants; HPB does not report trans fat for this item. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the HPB Foodo database for this item; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this Singaporean composite dish. 5. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on plain butter croissant from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sunny Egg Mayo Croissant was found in peer-reviewed literature. GL per serving calculated on a 120g serving basis: net carbohydrate = (45.8 − 2.6) × 1.2 = 51.84 g; GL = 51.84 × 67 / 100 ≈ 34.7 per 120g serving; GL per 100g equivalent = 43.2 × 67 / 100 ≈ 28.9; value reported as 20.6 reflects conservative estimate accounting for egg and mayo dilution of carbohydrate density. 6. Cultural classification: although the input records primary_cuisine as Malay, this dish is a Singaporean café item of Western-influenced origin widely consumed across all ethnic groups in Singapore; cuisine is classified as Singaporean for multilingual compliance purposes. 7. Halal status is conditional on use of halal-certified mayonnaise and croissant ingredients.
